questions.prompt: The DC load line x-intercept (IC=0) is at VCE =
questions.explanation: When IC=0: VCE = VCC − 0 = VCC. All supply voltage appears across the transistor.

questions.prompt: The DC load line y-intercept (VCE=0) is at IC =
questions.explanation: When VCE=0: IC = VCC/(RC+RE). All supply voltage drops across the resistors.

questions.prompt: The DC load line uses total resistance:
questions.explanation: DC equivalent: caps open, so both RC and RE carry the full DC current. Total R = RC + RE.

questions.prompt: The AC load line slope is −1/RAC where RAC =
questions.explanation: For AC: bypass cap shorts RE, coupling cap connects RL. Effective AC load = RC || RL.

questions.prompt: The AC load line is steeper than the DC load line because:
questions.explanation: RAC = RC||RL < RC+RE = RDC. Steeper slope = larger magnitude = −1/(smaller R).

questions.prompt: Both DC and AC load lines pass through:
questions.explanation: The Q-point is the DC operating point where both lines intersect. It's the centre of the AC swing.

questions.prompt: To construct the output waveform graphically, you read VCE at:
options: IB extremes (max and min) along the AC load line
questions.correctAnswer: IB extremes (max and min) along the AC load line
questions.explanation: The AC signal swings IB above and below IBQ. Read VCE where these IB curves intersect the AC load line.

questions.prompt: Clipping at saturation occurs when VCE drops to:
questions.explanation: In saturation, both junctions are forward-biased and VCE ≈ 0.2V. The output can't go lower than this.

questions.prompt: Clipping at cutoff occurs when IC drops to:
questions.explanation: In cutoff, IC ≈ 0 and VCE swings up to VCC (DC) or VCEQ + ICQ·RAC (AC). No further swing possible.

questions.prompt: The AC load line x-intercept (iC=0) equals:
questions.explanation: Starting from Q-point, when IC drops to 0: VCE increases by ICQ × RAC. So x-intercept = VCEQ + ICQ × RAC.
